entries.name: Conventional Commits
entries.description: Force commit messages to follow the Conventional Commits spec (feat:, fix:, chore:, BREAKING CHANGE:).
entries.author: Woom defaults

source.content:
---
name: conventional-commits
description: When the user asks for a git commit, ALWAYS format the message using Conventional Commits. Subject line: <type>(<scope>): <description>. type ∈ {feat, fix, chore, refactor, docs, test, perf, build, ci, revert}. Use "!" or a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer for breaking changes. Body wraps at 72 cols and explains *why*, not *what*.
---

# Conventional Commits

Apply this skill whenever you draft a git commit message. Reject one-liner messages like "updated stuff" — restate the change in the required shape:

```
<type>(<scope>): <subject in imperative present tense>

<body — wrap at 72 cols, explain motivation>

<footers — Closes #123, BREAKING CHANGE: ...>
```

## Type quick-ref

- **feat** — new user-facing feature
- **fix** — bug fix
- **chore** — tooling, deps, no production impact
- **refactor** — code change without behaviour change
- **docs** — documentation only
- **test** — tests only
- **perf** — performance work
- **build** — build system / packaging
- **ci** — CI pipeline config
- **revert** — reverts a previous commit

If the change crosses multiple types, pick the dominant one and mention the others in the body.

entries.name: No trailing summary
entries.description: Stop ending responses with a recap of what was just changed — the diff already says it.
entries.author: Woom defaults

source.content:
---
name: no-trailing-summary
description: After making code changes, do NOT end the response with a paragraph that recaps every file you touched. The user can read the diff. One short sentence stating what changed (or just "done") is enough. Skip section headers like "## Summary" / "## Changes" in chat replies.
---

# No trailing summary

When you finish a task:
- ✅ "Done — `auth.ts:42` now retries on 5xx."
- ❌ "## Summary\n- Added retry logic to auth.ts\n- Updated tests\n- ...\n\n## Files changed\n- ..."

The diff IS the summary. Reserve recaps for PR descriptions, not chat turns.

entries.name: DB Migration Safety Check
entries.description: Before approving any SQL migration, audit it for lock-heavy operations, missing indexes, and irreversible changes.
entries.author: Woom defaults

source.content:
---
name: migration-safety
description: When you see a SQL migration file (e.g. `*.sql` under `migrations/`, `db/migrate/`, `prisma/migrations/`, alembic, etc.), run a safety pass BEFORE proposing it. Flag: (1) `ALTER TABLE … NOT NULL` on large tables without a default + backfill plan; (2) `CREATE INDEX` without `CONCURRENTLY` on Postgres; (3) renaming or dropping columns referenced by application code; (4) anything that takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on a hot table mid-traffic.
---

# Migration safety pass

For every migration you author or review, walk this checklist explicitly:

1. **Locks** — does this take ACCESS EXCLUSIVE / SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE on a table with concurrent writes? On Postgres prefer `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`, `ALTER TABLE … ADD COLUMN` with a constant default (Postgres ≥11 fast-path), and split renames into add-new + backfill + drop-old across two deploys.
2. **Backfill** — if a column is becoming NOT NULL or gaining a non-constant default, the migration MUST ship with a backfill step. Don't rely on row-by-row UPDATE in the same transaction.
3. **Reversibility** — for irreversible ops (DROP TABLE, DROP COLUMN, type narrowing) confirm the user understands rollback is impossible without a restore. Suggest a soft-delete or rename-to-_deprecated step instead.
4. **App coupling** — if the migration drops or renames a column, grep the repo for references first. Mention any caller still using the old name in your review.

Report findings as a numbered list and propose concrete fixes; don't just say "this might be risky".

entries.name: Test before claiming done
entries.description: Don't say "all good / done" until you've actually run tests or exercised the feature. Code that compiles isn't code that works.
entries.author: Woom defaults

source.content:
---
name: test-before-claim-done
description: When you finish a code change, before reporting it as complete, RUN the test suite OR exercise the feature manually. Typechecking / build passing is not the same as the feature working. If you cannot run tests in this environment, SAY SO explicitly — don't bluff with "should work".
---

# Test before claiming done

Never end a task with "all good!" / "done!" / "this should work" unless one of the following is true:

1. You ran the relevant test command and it passed.
2. You exercised the feature end-to-end (CLI invocation, UI click-through, API request).
3. You explicitly state: "I haven't run tests because <reason>. The change typechecks but is unverified."

Type-checks and builds pass for many broken programs. The user is the one whose time gets wasted when you bluff.

entries.name: Anthropic example skills
entries.description: Cookbook-style example skills from Anthropic's public repo. Browse what's in the repo and pick what you need.
entries.author: Anthropic
entries.note: This installs the whole cookbooks repo into ~/.claude/skills/anthropic-examples. It's not a single skill — treat it as a reference folder. Delete and replace with a tighter ref once you know what you want.
